Text: Why is an artist's music often perceived as "cheapened" by having a predominantly teenage female fanbase? Why do people treat an artist's music as less valuable simply because it's popular among teenage girls? I've noticed that for many artists, the moment a predominantly teenage female fanbase becomes associated with them, people start dismissing the music itself, as though the audience somehow reduces the artistic value of the work. What I find particularly strange is the assumption that teenage girls liking something is evidence against its quality. Teenagers often experience emotions very intensely and tend to form deep personal connections with music. If a piece of music resonates strongly with a demographic that is actively seeking emotional expression, why is that treated as a sign that the music is shallow, rather than a sign that it has connected with its audience? Why are teenage girls' preferences so often used as a metric for judging the worth of music? (Before anyone wonders — Yes, I am a 19 year old female. And no, I am neither a Swiftie, nor a K-pop fan.) The funniest part of this thread is that a surprising number of people seem convinced I'm secretly trying to defend my own music taste. I explicitly stated that I'm not a fan of those artists because I foresaw exactly this happening: a crowd of people rushing in to explain why the artists are bad, while completely sidestepping the point I was trying to discuss. What's especially ironic is that my own music taste is quite far removed from the mainstream pop music and fandoms that people keep assuming I'm talking about. The reality is that my personal taste has very little to do with the question. Even if I listened exclusively to music that fell completely outside the stereotypes people associate with teenage girls, I'd still be asking the same thing: Why is "teenage girls like it" so often used as evidence that something is artistically inferior? At times it feels like people are arguing with a stereotype they've invented rather than the question I actually asked.
Label: analysis

Text: I think you're looking at it from the wrong angle. It's not necessarily that people dismiss an artist's music because teenage girls like it. The bigger factor is that many artists with predominantly teenage female fanbases were deliberately designed and marketed to appeal to that demographic in the first place. For decades, the music industry has created acts specifically aimed at young teenage girls, often prioritizing image, marketability, and emotional appeal over artistic innovation. As a result, critics sometimes associate those artists with a more commercial or "manufactured" approach to music. There are obviously exceptions. Bands like The Beatles or The Rolling Stones attracted huge numbers of teenage girls, but they weren't originally assembled by record labels as products for that audience. Their popularity among teenage girls was a consequence of their success, not the reason they existed. So the stereotype isn't really "teenage girls like it, therefore it's cheap." It's more that many acts that are perceived as manufactured happen to be targeted primarily at teenage girls, and people often confuse the audience with the marketing strategy behind the artist.
Label: analysis

Text: Here's the thing; I think what's lost in this discussion, and what many tend to forget, is rock n' roll was a new medium. There were no grizzled old faded rockers back in the late 50's and early 60's. It was all teenagers that liked it. Adults hated it, and they saw it as a threat. That's where the term "generation gap" actually stems from. Your parents were listening to Frank Sinatra and Bing Crosby, and if you were a kid in the 60's you were listening to the Beatles and the Stones. In the 50's you were listening t Elvis, Little Richard and Chuck Berry. Go watch The Buddy Holly Story, and you'll see that in the first act the preacher is talking about how "this jungle rhythm" was a threat to society.
Label: analysis

Text: There were no grizzled old faded rockers back in the late 50's and early 60's. It was all teenagers that liked it. I think it's worth noting that this is very much the 'small town white teenager' version of history - there were plenty of grizzled old faded rockers in the 50s, they were just invisible to that audience because they had been famous in pretty much exclusively adult, urban, black communities in the decades prior. Big Joe Turner, Roy Brown, Bessie Smith, Guitar Floyd, Louis Jordan, Jimmy Preston etc. You know, all the guys Bill Haley was ripping off and sanitizing for the suburban sock hop crowd. These guys all get a bit of a raw deal, with the Boomer version of Rock history insisting on starting in 1954 when these guys had been calling the style of R&B music they were playing 'rock and roll' since (at the latest) 1938.
Label: analysis

Text: Your second to last paragraph is closer to what I believe OP is getting at. It often doesn't matter if the popularity with teen girls was a consequence and not the reason, as the story gets re-written in the popular consciousness. It's not necessarily that people dismiss an artist's music because teenage girls like it I'm going to use the band Sleep Token as what I believe to be an excellent modern example of this. In the late 2010s, they were the darlings of the UK prog metal scene. You can still find posts from old Facebook groups and independent review blogs where important figures in the scene talk about Sleep Token with a rare kind of excitement and respect. When they played Tech-Fest in 2018, people in the know were hailing them as the most interesting thing to happen to prog in a long time. By the time their first album dropped in 2019, they had a core fanbase of metalheads, whether or not the band were metal themselves. Cut to 2026 - Any article posted about Sleep Token is littered with insults, accusations of being industry plants, and vitriolic comparison to manufactured pop artists. There is no faster way to irritate a metalhead than to mention (groan) fucking Sleep Token. So what happened in the intervening time? Why, they became extremely popular with young women and teenage girls, of course! So what has changed about the band since 2019? Did they completely "sell out" and change their sound/image for this demographic? Here are some reasons young girls in particular might like Sleep Token: Shirtless frontman in a mask Erotic/dark lyrics that imply sexuality but don't treat women as objects Themes of self-doubt and needing to grow/change Hopeless romanticism Gentle pop-like sections Things that were already present in 2019: Literally all of the above Their sound has diversified a bit but is broadly the same: Piano-driven ballads (Compare 'Blood Sport' 2019 to 'Damocles' 2025) Urban beats and rap-like lyrical delivery (Compare 'Dark Signs' 2019 to 'Gethsemane' 2025) Gentle singing followed by heavy emotional bursts (Compare 'Jericho' 2017 to 'Chokehold' 2023) Heavy (Compare 'Gods' 2019 to 'Vore' 2023) Granted, there will be plenty of people who simply don't like this band, and people whose tastes changed. But there is also a large group of people who will swear blind that the band "sold out" and "became a completely different band". That there was once something respectable which has been fundamentally altered. There are also lots of people who refuse to check Sleep Token out at all because they're "girl music" and therefore fake/non-art/boys who go on posters. Teenage girls are cringe and anything they like must therefore, by extension, be cringe. It's really just... regular ol' misogyny.
Label: analysis

Text: It's just good old fashioned sexism and it's not exclusive to music either. Movies with a large female audience also tend to get derided. There's even perjorative terms like women's weepers or chick flicks. The reason for this is actually quite simple: film criticism has long been and continue to be dominated by men. It's not a coincidence that the vast majority of movies that are widely heralded as classics are the sort of thing that would appeal to those male critics. Have you noticed that there's very few female characters in movies like The Godfather, Goodfellas, Apocalypse Now, Saving Private Ryan, The Dark Knight, Fight Club, The Matrix, 12 Angry Men, The Shawshank Redemption, Jaws, Schindler's List, Star Wars, Seven Samurai, Lawrence of Arabia, The Bridge on the River Kwai, 2001: A Space Odyssey, A Clockwork Orange, etc.? Heck, there are numerous acclaimed directors that rarely make movies about women, like Akira Kurosawa, Martin Scorsese, Wes Anderson, Christopher Nolan, etc. Music criticism has also long been and continue to be dominated by men. It's not a coincidence either than all those lists of the greatest music artists are always dominated by men: The Beatles, The Rolling Stones, The Beach Boys, Pink Floyd, Led Zeppelin, Queen, Jimi Hendrix, Chuck Berry, Elvis Presley, Bob Dylan, Johnny Cash, Willie Nelson, Hank Williams, Waylon Jennings, Eminem, Jay-Z, Tupac Shakur, The Notorious B.I.G., Kendrick Lamar, Michael Jackson, David Bowie, Bruce Springsteen, James Brown, Ray Charles, Nirvana, Radiohead, Oasis, Miles Davis, John Coltrane, Duke Ellington, Bob Marley, etc. Now don't get me wrong. I'm not saying that any of these movies or music artists are bad. And sure, we can all think of exceptions like Alien, Titanic, Dolly Parton, or Joni Mitchell but it would be much more difficult for anyone to come up with a list of 100 great movies without any male leads than 100 great movies without any female leads. It's not that there aren't enough of the latter but they're not widely known and very few are widely regarded as "classics" like the aforementioned titles. This discrepancy is even more apparent when it comes to music because there obviously are hundreds and hundreds of well-known female singers or musicians. Yet most people would still struggle to create a list of 100 great music artists consisting entirely of women while it would be easy for them to create such a list consisting only of men. An a broader level, genres that are associated as feminine like melodramas, romantic comedies, disco, or dance pop tend to get overlooked or dismissed as lightweight and frivolous, not worthy of serious discussion or evaluation. As a result, even men associated with these genres tend to be regarded lowly, like Douglas Sirk and Garry Marshall in movies or pretty much any boy band in music. Who gets to decide what's worthy? Sure, any of us can do so ourselves - and we really ought to - but on a societal level, critics have a far greater influence and, to repeat myself once again, the fields of film and music criticism have long been and continue to be dominated by men. It's really telling when a publication like Pitchfork can ignore Taylor Swift for years yet didn't hesitate to review a cover album of her music that was recorded by a lesser known male indie-rock singer, Ryan Adams. Sure, it was a bad review but she was already a far bigger name back then yet she wasn't considered worthy of being reviewed while a man recording her songs was. Now think of how many lesser known female artists get ignored by male critics who think them as unworthy of even a bad review.
Label: analysis

Text: To answer this question, we have to begin with an honest consideration of which genres are most likely to be held in highest regard by the most people: Baroque/Classical/Romantic Blues/Jazz/Swing Anything decent that’s at least one generation out-of-date So what has your demographic loved in past decades? ‘50s Elvis: hated by parents, loved by teenagers. Now his works are Rock & Roll canon. ‘60s Beatles: dismissed when first popular, now revered as a musical deity. ‘70s ABBA: teenage disco fluff when popular, now renowned and studied in the pop world. ‘80s Duran Duran: seen as manufactured pretty boys when popular, now recognized as extremely talented musicians (and singer) who were judged unfairly at the time. ‘90s Backstreet Boys & NSYNC: only just starting to get more credit now that enough time has passed. Former frat boys will unironically belt out some of their songs at Karaoke. ‘00s Britney Spears: talk about a redemption arc in the public’s eye… The pattern seems to be that enough time needs to pass for the act in question to shed the social baggage of being rabidly adored by the youth, since the people who eventually come around to this music that they’d previously dismissed want to distance themselves from the original fan base and culture. We’ll see which current artists end up standing the test of time in a few decades. My money’s on Lady Gaga being cherished in retrospect by generations to come, like Queen and Madonna. The comparison might sound wild now, but give it time…
Label: analysis

Text: Misogyny and the belief by some people that their appreciation of music is unaffected by looks. A lot of spaces like the printed music press (Rolling Stone etc.) and music sites (Pitchfork and similar) are dominated by men over 25+ (and often much older), who see themselves as having an omniscient view of music, which rests purely on the audio and is unaffected by any kind of visuals / backstory / charisma of the artist etc. From that viewpoint, the idea that teenage girls are into an artist is a negative, because surely they are only valuing good looks, dance routines, the outfits the artists wear, and other such nonsense. Once you strip away all of that, the music is probably not worth listening to. To me, the reality is that music is very rarely enjoyed in a vacuum and some of the biggest cultural and commercial successes have had great images. Think of Bon Iver's "For Emma..." which was a massive success partly because a lot of people loved the backstory + image (recording in a cabin in the woods, singer with a nice beard and backwoods clothes), Daft Punk's slick robot helmets and live shows, Gorillaz entire comic book presentation etc. It's also worth noting that most rock acts have very good looking lead singers (Oasis, Nirvana, Pearl Jam etc.). I think there is also a big gap between the emotional life of a middle aged man and a teenage girl- and most middle aged men have no interest in bridging that gap with empathy.
Label: analysis

Text: I have to say, I'm really disappointed in a lot of the takes here. Some are insightful but too many are just being lazy and crying misogyny. Criticism for something women like is not misogyny. It would be disliking it purely because women like it. I think there are far better explanations available if we use our brains for a minute. I don't think it's controversial to say that in music authenticity is valued. It's not always valued to the same degree and it's certainly nebulous what it really means. But artists who make their own way definitely get more respect than those who are supported by the industry. If you compare Billie Eilish to One Direction, one gets a lot more respect than the other despite the fact that both are popular with younger audiences. Billie's music is written and produced by herself and her brother, at least initially I'm not sure if it still is. But also initially it was done at home on a PC. One Direction was purely a creation of the industry, and it shows. Not every act that is popular with teenagers is like One Direction. The Beatles started on their own and when they were picked up by the industry they were initially marketed that way. That's not something they set out to do, it's something their label overlords picked for them. And I think it's telling that both the Beatles and Harry Styles did a lot to get away from that. The Beatles are of course the Beatles. But I haven't heard the same criticism of Harry since he went solo. So yeah authenticity is a big part of it and the nature of the industry and also how teenagers access popular culture means they really can't make their own choices. They are too young to go to shows on their own. They don't have income so they aren't buying their own music. And they are also more impressionable and suggestable. The industry takes advantage of this and pushes acts they either find or create for the most effect. This has broken down a bit since streaming became a thing but Spotify is just as if not more manipulated than the radio ever was. So you have a demographic who haven't been listening to music long. Haven't really developed their own tastes. Are a largely captive audience. And are also at the age when emotions are felt stronger and they can't regulate them, thanks to puberty. Inauthentic artists who are intentionally marketed to that demographic are naturally getting less respect than one who are authentic and happen to still be appealing to that audience. And they will get less respect than artists who are authentic and write music that appeals to a more mature audience.
Label: analysis

Text: Oh, absolutely. I'm glad someone well associated with the "real music community" asked for an example. The reason I ended up making this post in the first place was because I recently came across someone talking about how her boyfriend genuinely loves Mitski's music, listens to her regularly, and connects with it on a fan level, yet refuses to call himself a Mitski fan because he perceives her fanbase as being made up of girls aged 13–15. The music wasn't the issue. His enjoyment of the music wasn't the issue. The problem was the demographic he associated with the fandom. That's the phenomenon I'm talking about. I'm not arguing that every artist with a teenage female fanbase is good, nor am I arguing that every criticism of an artist is rooted in sexism. I'm asking why so many people seem to view sharing an interest with teenage girls as inherently embarrassing or as something that diminishes the legitimacy of that interest. And this isn't exactly a rare occurrence. People have spent years dismissing things like boy bands, romance novels, fandom culture, fanfiction communities, and various pop artists with arguments that often boil down to "teenage girls like it." In many cases, the audience becomes part of the criticism. If a person can thoroughly enjoy something in private but suddenly feels that their maturity, credibility, or taste is threatened once they realize teenage girls enjoy it too, then I think it's worth asking what exactly they're measuring maturity against. Because at that point, we're no longer talking about the quality of the art. We're talking about the social status of the people consuming it.
Label: analysis

Text: This is a pretty interesting topic. Reading this, I can see some of the different nuances. On the one hand, making art that is enjoyed by teenage girls should not be a mark against the artist. Some have talked about how different genres of music and artists were dismissed ranging from the early Beatles, Girl Groups, different types of pop music and pop stars, good looking artists. Over time, people have been able to recognize the artistic merit of these artists. On the other hand, artists who are appealing to teenage girls in a more predatory way casts different undertones to the music. And there's also the distinction people have between being widely appealing and manufacturing your music to be widely appealing. In the broad sense, I know there's the discussion on children's media vs general audience media vs teenager media vs adult media. All of these categories can have artistic merit. But at different age groups, it's expected that you'll enjoy the media that is most appropriate. Some of this is valid: enjoying only children's media could cause you to miss out on art that presents a more nuanced perspectives and experiences. It's important to broaden one's palette. But you also can't just put adult themes into children's media before they're ready. Because that can also distort one's childhood experiences and their sense of boundaries. I actually think there's quite a bit to sift through and hope we can discuss it more.
Label: analysis

Text: Misogyny. That’s the answer. I know you’re not a Swiftie, OP, and neither am I, but I started looking a bit more closely Taylor Swift’s success a while ago and this is what I noticed. Take a close look at the main similarities between Taylor Swift and a more “serious” male artist - let’s go with Bruce Springsteen. Both are very successful singer-songwriters, obviously. Both are excellent lyrical storytellers; most of their songs tell interesting, sometimes quite dark and moving stories, that are not necessarily autobiographical, many in which they are playing characters, the protagonists. Both have had big hits with catchy, lighter “pop” singles, eg Springsteen with “Dancing in the Dark”, “Hungry Heart”, for example. Swift with most of her early song catalogue. Both have also done more serious and introspective acoustic-leaning work, Swift with Folklore and Evermore, Springsteen with The River and Nebraska. Now I will gladly die on the hill that they are incredibly similar artists. Yet Springsteen gets revered as a musical genius and the voice of a generation; Swift is dismissed by the same critics as “music for teenage girls”. Swift’s massive success and popularity can only be tempered by making her work seem trivial and less significant, even though the only real difference is…her audience. This is misogyny at work.
Label: analysis

Text: Teenage girls literally control the music industry. Its been a thing since the first time Elvis thrusted his hips on TV and they cut to a teenage girl swooning over him. The formula is quite simple. Teenagers (no offense) are gullible, they are easily manipulated and succumb to peer pressure relatively easily. If you want to capture the largest portion of the teenage demographic, then all you need to do is get girls to go, and the boys will simply follow. If you market to teenage boys, you don't usually get too many girls. Teenagers also have no responsibilities, they don't care how they spend their money because they don't often have to work for it, and many are on an allowance. Finally, a majority of people stop consuming new music in their late teens and early 20s, so marketing music to an adult crowd tends to not be as lucrative as marketing to teens. Teens, again no offense, are more apt to become super fans because their world experience is a lot more narrow, and in a world of trying to fit in, and make sense of the pit falls of high school while also figuring out who you are as a person, when you find something you feel represents you, there's a much stronger connection as a teen. So when music is obviously just marketed to teenage girls, it can be hard to take seriously as fan of music as an artform more than a product. The late 90s and early 00s were prime examples in fairly recent times. The "boy band era." N'Sync, 98 degrees, Backstreet Boys etc. Great performers, sure. But nothing about them was actually targeted to boys, it was all designed with the idea of getting young girls interested in them, so the boys who were interested in the girls would follow. And given the music industry's extensive past of grooming and pedophilia, there's an extra layer of gross attached to it to me when grown ass men are writing music for other men to perform that is targeted at a teenage girl demographic.
Label: analysis

Text: Broadly speaking - the teen girl demographic cares more about how music makes them feel than about artistic merit - so they’re more easily marketed to by inauthentic pop acts (like manufactured boy bands) who can target those emotional buttons with minimal artistic merit. It’s about prioritising feeling and not technical musical greatness - so the artistic bar is lower. Then people observe that trend and say ‘their taste is cheap’. Broadly speaking - guys tend to be more in awe of technical musical skill and consider what it requires of an artist to create great authentic art; they’re more likely to debate the great guitar soloists, rap lyricists, classic albums etc because men prioritise competence and innately recognise hierarchy, even in music, to be significant. They do it with sports and comedy, they care about technical accomplishment - so the artistic bar is higher. Teen girls dont tend to debate those things simply because they value them less than how much an artist pushes their emotional buttons - desire, fantasy, inspiration etc - if they do that job, even if it’s a manufactured pop group, it’s met the threshold of what matters. That doesn’t imply teen girls dont like great artists, it just means they may tend to like them for different reasons to guys. So that’s why, IMO, theres a difference between guys and girls tastes - but why are girls judged for that difference? Because of course it’s the men who are the ones doing the judging - men’s tendency to rate everything by hierarchy and competence (less on emotion alone) dominate discussions of art.
Label: analysis

Text:
I can understand why my comment gave you that impression. However, once this post started gaining traction, I received a lot of responses from people who brought perspectives and experiences that I simply don't have. I've been making a genuine effort to read through them carefully and with an open mind, and I've come across several insights that I found valuable and hadn't considered before. I've even made a point of thanking people for sharing their perspectives when they approached the discussion with a degree of skepticism or criticism, because the whole reason I made this post was to hear viewpoints that differed from my own.

When I said, "I wish I could find a valid explanation other than sexism or misogyny for once," I don't think I expressed myself very clearly. What I meant was that I made this post precisely because I wanted to explore explanations that went beyond those frameworks. I was hoping to discover different angles, challenge my own assumptions, and better understand a phenomenon that I've been thinking about for a long time.

You don't have to take my word for it, but that has genuinely been my intention throughout this discussion :)
Label: anecdote

Text:
I would distinguish, though, that those aren't Blues guys, they are Rock guys. There were old Blues guys, too, certainly.

The urban Jump Blues/R&B scene that birthed Rock and Roll during Prohibition shared 'blue notes' but wasn't really in conversation with Blues, the genre, for the most part (which was more of a rural folk tradition), until you get to get into the mid 50s, the result of both genres moving towards the electric guitar and crossover artists like Howlin' Wolf.

That Elvis story sounds a bit apocryphal to me - while he did cover a couple songs by black artists (famously, lol), Elvis's early Sun 'Rockabilly' era is pretty so deeply Western Swing that it really doesn't sound like black music at the time, to my ears, at least, until 1956, a year after he first performed with Holly. But, of course, impossible to know what sounded like what in Texas 70 years ago.
Label: analysis

Text:
Again, you're judging it by today's standards. A kid in Lubbock is not going to know what Elvis looks like if he only hears him on the RADIO.

And black musicians were playing rhythm and blues. Not "western swing." Guys like Eddie Cochran and Buddy Holly took a lot their country and western roots, and combined it with R & B. Tour poster didn't go up until maybe a couple of days before a show.

The reason why you think the early history of rock n' roll is "bullshit," is because you're overthinking things and you think that the world you see around you as always existing. "Oh, how can you not know what Elvis look liked?" If you're from a relatively small town in the Middle of the country with one newspaper and you had to go down to the town center and watch Ed Sullivan on a TV in a department store window, which a lot people did, then the chances of seeing Elvis before buying his records or seeing him live were very small, until he was in his first movie "Love Me Tender." John Lennon said the first time he saw Elvis was at the movies. Many 45s were still being shipped in plain paper sleeves until the mid 50's with no artwork on them.

This reminds me of when I told someone about the tragedy of the band Badfinger, how their manager ripped them off, and Warner Bros pulled their album "Wish You Were Here," and refused to release their next album. Someone asked me "why couldn't they release their music themselves?" I told him it was 1974 and that wasn't an option. They couldn't grasp there was no way for you to do that back then, because a world without the internet or things like YouTube and Spotify didn't seem possible. It's not "boomer history," it's just history.
Label: analysis
